rounds.title: I need a high-performance embedded key-value storage engine built in Go from scratch, usin
rounds.md:
I need a high-performance embedded key-value storage engine built in Go from scratch, using an LSM-Tree (Log-Structured Merge-Tree) architecture with full MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) support, optimistic transactions, and advanced query capabilities. The engine must be accessible through a command-line interface that reads commands from stdin (one per line) and writes results to stdout. ## CLI Interface The binary should be buildable via `go build -o kvstore ./cmd/kvstore` from the `/app` directory. It accepts a `--data-dir ` flag (default: `./data`) pointing to the directory where all persistent state is stored. ### Basic Commands - **`PUT `** — Insert or update a key-value pair outside any transaction (auto-commit). Keys are single tokens (no whitespace). Values are everything after the key, trimmed of leading/trailing whitespace. Output: `OK ` where seqnum is the assigned sequence number. - **`GET `** — Retrieve the latest committed value. Output: the value string if found, or `NOT_FOUND`. - **`DELETE `** — Mark a key as deleted via point tombstone (auto-commit). Output: `OK `. - **`DELETE_RANGE `** — Mark all keys in the inclusive lexicographic range [start_key, end_key] as deleted via a range tombstone (auto-commit). Output: `OK `. - **`SCAN [flags]`** — Inclusive lexicographic range scan on the latest committed state. Output one line per matching entry: `\t` (tab-separated). Flags: - `--prefix

` — Only return keys starting with prefix `p` (applied within the [start_key, end_key] range). - `--limit ` — Return at most N entries. - `--reverse` — Return entries in reverse lexicographic order. - Flags can be combined in any order. Output nothing if the result is empty. Deleted keys (by point or range tombstone) must not appear. - **`FLUSH`** — Force the current MemTable to be written to disk as an SSTable. Output: `OK`. - **`COMPACT`** — Trigger a full compaction across all levels. Output: `OK`. - **`STATS`** — Output engine statistics as `key=value` pairs, one per line. Must include at minimum: `memtable_size`, `l0_sstable_count`, `total_sstable_count`, `total_keys` (live keys excluding tombstoned/expired), `wal_size`, `current_seqnum` (latest assigned sequence number), `open_snapshots` (number of active snapshots), `open_transactions` (number of active transactions). ### MVCC Snapshot Commands - **`SNAPSHOT`** — Create a read-only snapshot at the current committed sequence number. Output: `SNAP:` where id is a monotonically increasing integer starting at 1. - **`SNAP_GET `** — Read a key from the given snapshot. Only mutations with sequence number <= the snapshot's sequence number are visible. Output: value or `NOT_FOUND`. - **`SNAP_SCAN [flags]`** — Range scan from the given snapshot. Same flags as SCAN. Only data committed at or before the snapshot's sequence number is visible. - **`SNAP_RELEASE `** — Release a snapshot. Output: `OK`. After release, the snapshot ID is invalid and further operations on it return `ERROR: invalid snapshot`. ### Transaction Commands Transactions use optimistic concurrency control (OCC). Each transaction operates on a private snapshot taken at BEGIN time, with buffered writes. - **`BEGIN`** — Start a new transaction. Output: `TX:` where id is a monotonically increasing integer starting at 1. - **`TX_PUT `** — Buffer a PUT within the transaction. Output: `OK`. - **`TX_DELETE `** — Buffer a DELETE within the transaction. Output: `OK`. - **`TX_DELETE_RANGE `** — Buffer a range DELETE within the transaction. Output: `OK`. - **`TX_GET `** — Read a key within the transaction. Sees the transaction's own buffered writes overlaid on its snapshot. Output: value or `NOT_FOUND`. - **`TX_SCAN [flags]`** — Scan within the transaction. Sees buffered writes overlaid on snapshot. Same flags as SCAN. - **`COMMIT `** — Attempt to commit the transaction. Uses optimistic conflict detection: if any key written by this transaction was also written (committed) by another transaction after this transaction's snapshot sequence number, the commit fails. Output: `OK ` on success (seqnum is the commit sequence number), or `CONFLICT` on conflict. On conflict, the transaction is automatically rolled back. - **`ROLLBACK `** — Discard all buffered writes. Output: `OK`. A committed transaction's writes become visible atomically — they all share the same sequence number and are written to the WAL as a single batch entry. Invalid transaction IDs (already committed, rolled back, or non-existent) return `ERROR: invalid transaction`. ### Write Batch Command For atomic multi-key writes without full transaction overhead: - **`BATCH `** — Start reading a write batch of `count` operations. The next `count` lines must each be either `PUT ` or `DELETE ` or `DELETE_RANGE `. All operations in the batch are written atomically to the WAL and applied with the same sequence number. Output after all operations are read: `OK `. ### Error Handling The tool must handle EOF on stdin gracefully by flushing any pending MemTable data and exiting with code 0. Unknown commands produce `ERROR: unknown command` on stdout and continue processing. Commands with wrong argument counts produce `ERROR: wrong number of arguments` and continue. ## Storage Architecture ### Sequence Numbers and MVCC Every mutation (PUT, DELETE, DELETE_RANGE) is assigned a monotonically increasing sequence number starting at 1. The sequence number is persisted in the WAL and SSTables. Multiple mutations in a single batch or transaction commit share the same sequence number. For reads: - Non-transactional GET/SCAN always read at the latest committed sequence number. - Snapshot reads see only mutations with seq <= snapshot_seq. - Transaction reads see their own buffered writes plus the snapshot at BEGIN time. Compaction must preserve entries that are still visible to any open snapshot. An entry with sequence number S can only be garbage collected if: (a) there is a newer entry for the same key, AND (b) S is less than the minimum sequence number of all open snapshots, AND (c) the newer entry also has seq <= min_snapshot_seq (so the snapshot would see the newer version instead). If no snapshots are open, standard compaction rules apply (keep only newest entry per key). ### Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Every mutation must be durably appended to the WAL before acknowledgment. The WAL supports two entry types: **Single entry:** | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | entry_type | uint8 | 1 = single | | op | uint8 | 1 = PUT, 2 = DELETE, 3 = DELETE_RANGE | | seqnum | uint64 LE | Sequence number | | key_len | uint32 LE | Key length | | key | []byte | Key | | val_len | uint32 LE | Value length (0 for DELETE; for DELETE_RANGE: end_key length) | | value | []byte | Value (for DELETE_RANGE: end_key) | | crc | uint32 LE | CRC-32 IEEE over all preceding fields | **Batch entry** (for write batches and transaction commits): | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | entry_type | uint8 | 2 = batch | | seqnum | uint64 LE | Shared sequence number | | op_count | uint32 LE | Number of operations | | ops | []op | Sequence of operations, each: op(uint8) + key_len(uint32 LE) + key + val_len(uint32 LE) + value | | crc | uint32 LE | CRC-32 IEEE over all preceding fields | On startup, replay the WAL. Entries with CRC mismatch are discarded and replay stops at the first corrupted entry. After successful MemTable flush, truncate the WAL. ### MemTable In-memory sorted data structure holding recent writes. Each entry stores: key, value, sequence number, operation type (put/delete/delete_range). When total data size (sum of key+value byte lengths) reaches or exceeds **4096 bytes**, auto-flush to Level-0 SSTable. Range tombstones in the MemTable are stored as special entries with the start_key as the key and end_key as the value, marked with op type DELETE_RANGE. During reads, range tombstones must be checked: a key K is covered by a range tombstone [S, E] if S <= K <= E and the range tombstone's sequence number is greater than K's latest PUT sequence number. ### SSTable File Format Each SSTable is named `_.sst` (e.g., `000042_0.sst`). File layout: 1. **Data section**: Blocks of ~4096 bytes. Each entry in a block: - `key_len` (uint32 LE) + `key` + `val_len` (uint32 LE) + `value` + `op_type` (uint8: 1=put, 2=delete, 3=delete_range) + `seqnum` (uint64 LE) 2. **Index section**: Per-block index entries: - `first_key_len` (uint32 LE) + `first_key` + `block_offset` (uint64 LE) + `block_size` (uint32 LE) 3. **Bloom filter section**: Serialized bloom filter (k=7, m=10 bits per key) for point keys only (not range tombstone endpoints). 4. **Range tombstone section**: All range tombstones in this SSTable, sorted by start_key: - `count` (uint32 LE) - For each: `start_key_len` (uint32 LE) + `start_key` + `end_key_len` (uint32 LE) + `end_key` + `seqnum` (uint64 LE) 5. **Footer** (76 bytes): - `index_offset` (uint64 LE), `index_size` (uint32 LE) - `bloom_offset` (uint64 LE), `bloom_size` (uint32 LE) - `range_tomb_offset` (uint64 LE), `range_tomb_size` (uint32 LE) - `key_count` (uint64 LE), `data_size` (uint64 LE) - `min_seqnum` (uint64 LE), `max_seqnum` (uint64 LE) - `magic` (uint64 LE) = `0x4C534D5453535400` ### Read Path **GET**: Check MemTable first (including checking range tombstones). Then SSTables from newest to oldest. For each SSTable, check bloom filter, then check range tombstones, then search data blocks. Return the first match with highest sequence number. If match is a point or range tombstone, return NOT_FOUND. For snapshot reads, only consider entries with seq <= snapshot_seq. **SCAN**: Merge results from MemTable and all SSTables using a heap-based merge iterator. For each key, keep only the entry with the highest sequence number (within the visible range for snapshot reads). Apply range tombstone filtering: exclude keys covered by range tombstones with higher sequence numbers. Apply --prefix, --limit, --reverse flags. Results in strict lexicographic order (or reverse if --reverse). ### Compaction Level-based compaction (same as basic LSM): - L0: direct MemTable flushes, may overlap. Compact when 4+ L0 SSTables. - L0 compaction: merge all L0 with overlapping L1, target ~64KB per new L1 SSTable. - L1+: non-overlapping key ranges. Compact when level exceeds 10^L MB. - Range tombstones: during compaction, range tombstones must be preserved until it's certain they don't shadow any entries in deeper levels. A range tombstone can only be dropped during deepest-level compaction when no deeper level has keys in its range. - MVCC: entries visible to open snapshots must not be garbage collected (see MVCC rules above). - `COMPACT` forces full compaction regardless of thresholds. ### Crash Recovery On startup: 1. Delete `.tmp` SSTable files. 2. Load all `.sst` files, parse footers and indexes. 3. Replay WAL to reconstruct MemTable. 4. Rebuild snapshot/transaction state is lost on crash (snapshots and transactions are memory-only; clients must re-create them after restart). ## Correctness Requirements - All operations must be linearizable when accessed sequentially. - After graceful shutdown (EOF), all committed data is recoverable on restart. - SCAN produces strict lexicographic order (or reverse), no duplicates, no tombstoned entries. - Snapshot isolation: concurrent snapshots see consistent point-in-time views. - Transaction conflict detection: if two transactions write the same key and both try to commit, exactly one succeeds. - Write batches and transaction commits are atomic: either all mutations are visible or none. - Range tombstones correctly shadow all point entries in their range with lower sequence numbers. - Bloom filter empirical false positive rate below 5% for 1000+ keys. - Compaction never loses live data and respects MVCC visibility. Set up the project with `go.mod` at `/app/go.mod` using module path `kvstore`. Use only Go standard library packages.

rounds.title: I need key expiration support. Add a `--ttl <seconds>` flag to `PUT` — when set, the key e
rounds.md:
I need key expiration support. Add a `--ttl <seconds>` flag to `PUT` — when set, the key expires after that many seconds from the time of the write (use wall-clock time). Expired keys behave exactly like deleted keys: `GET` returns `NOT_FOUND`, `SCAN` excludes them, and compaction can GC them (respecting MVCC snapshot visibility — a snapshot taken before expiration still sees the key).

New command: `TTL <key>` — returns the remaining seconds until expiration (integer, rounded down), `-1` if the key has no TTL, or `NOT_FOUND` if the key doesn't exist.

`PUT` without `--ttl` on a key that previously had a TTL clears the TTL. `TX_PUT` and `BATCH PUT` also accept `--ttl <seconds>`.

`STATS` adds `expired_keys` (count of keys whose TTL has elapsed but haven't been GC'd yet). `total_keys` must exclude expired keys.

The TTL expiration timestamp must be persisted in the WAL and SSTables. For single WAL entries, add an `expire_at` field (uint64 LE, Unix timestamp in seconds, 0 = no expiration) immediately after `seqnum`. For batch WAL entries, each operation in the `ops` array becomes: `op` (uint8) + `expire_at` (uint64 LE) + `key_len` (uint32 LE) + `key` + `val_len` (uint32 LE) + `value`. SSTable data block entries add `expire_at` immediately after `seqnum`. The SSTable footer size does not change.

rounds.title: Two things this round.
rounds.md:
Two things this round.

First, there's a snapshot bug: if you write a bunch of keys, take a snapshot, write new versions of those keys, and enough flushes happen to trigger the L0-to-L1 compaction, the snapshot starts returning `NOT_FOUND` for keys it should still see. The full compaction path handles this correctly, so it's just the L0 compaction that's dropping versions it shouldn't. Fix that.

Second, add data block checksums to SSTables. Each data block should be followed by a CRC-32 (IEEE) checksum of the block's raw bytes, stored as a uint32 LE. The index entries' `block_size` should NOT include the checksum — the 4-byte checksum sits right after the block data. On read, verify the checksum and return `ERROR: data corruption detected` if it fails. Also add a `VERIFY` command that scans every SSTable file in the data directory: validates the footer magic, verifies every data block checksum, and re-checks the bloom filter and index integrity. Output `OK` if everything is clean, or one line per corrupted file: `CORRUPT: <filename> (<reason>)` followed by a final summary `VERIFY_FAILED: <N> file(s) corrupted`.

rounds.title: Add a merge operator. New command: `MERGE <key> <operand>` — appends the operand to the ke
rounds.md:
Add a merge operator. New command: `MERGE <key> <operand>` — appends the operand to the key's value as a comma-separated list. If the key already has a value from a previous PUT or MERGE, the new operand is appended with a comma separator. If the key doesn't exist or was deleted, the operand becomes the sole value. Multiple MERGEs stack: `MERGE k a`, `MERGE k b`, `MERGE k c` then `GET k` returns `a,b,c`. If a PUT preceded them — `PUT k x`, `MERGE k y`, `MERGE k z` — GET returns `x,y,z`. Output of MERGE: `OK <seqnum>`.

MERGE uses op_type 4 in the WAL and SSTable entries. The operand is stored in the value field. On the read path, collect all MERGE entries for a key back to the most recent PUT or DELETE (or the start of history), then fold them left-to-right with comma joining. This folding happens in GET, SCAN, snapshot reads, transaction reads, and anywhere else values are returned. `TX_MERGE <tx_id> <key> <operand>` and `BATCH MERGE <key> <operand>` work the same way. During compaction, a run of MERGE entries for the same key can be collapsed into a single entry only if no open snapshot needs to see the intermediate states. A run of MERGEs with no preceding PUT compacts to a single PUT with the folded value.

`STATS` adds `merge_operations` — total number of uncompacted MERGE entries across the memtable and all SSTables.

rounds.title: Two changes this round.
rounds.md:
Two changes this round.

First, add shared-prefix compression to SSTable data blocks. Within each data block, the first entry stores its full key using the existing format. Every subsequent entry stores a `shared_prefix_len` (uint16 LE) immediately before the `key_len` field, where `shared_prefix_len` is the number of leading bytes the key shares with the *previous* entry's full key. The `key_len` and `key` fields then contain only the non-shared suffix. So each non-first entry in a block is: `shared_prefix_len` (uint16 LE) + `key_len` (uint32 LE) + `key_suffix` + `val_len` (uint32 LE) + `value` + `op_type` (uint8) + `seqnum` (uint64 LE) + `expire_at` (uint64 LE). The first entry in each block has no `shared_prefix_len` field — the field is simply absent, not zero. The index section, bloom filter, footer, and range tombstone section are unchanged. The per-block CRC-32 checksum covers the compressed block bytes as written.

Second, SCAN now uses an **exclusive** end key. `SCAN a z` returns keys where key >= `a` AND key < `z`, not key <= `z`. This applies to SCAN, SNAP_SCAN, and TX_SCAN. The `--reverse` flag still reverses output order but does not change the range boundaries. DELETE_RANGE keeps its inclusive end boundaries — this is intentional, do not change DELETE_RANGE.

rounds.title: Add server-side cursors. `CURSOR_OPEN [flags]` creates a cursor backed by a snapshot taken
rounds.md:
Add server-side cursors. `CURSOR_OPEN [flags]` creates a cursor backed by a snapshot taken at open time. Output: `CURSOR:` where id is a monotonically increasing integer starting at 1. The cursor starts positioned *before* the first key. Flags: `--prefix

` restricts the cursor to keys with that prefix, `--reverse` flips the direction so CURSOR_NEXT goes backward and CURSOR_PREV goes forward. `CURSOR_NEXT ` — advance one position, output `\t`, or `END` if exhausted. `CURSOR_PREV ` — move back one position, output `\t`, or `END` if before the first key. `CURSOR_SEEK ` — position at the smallest key >= seek key (or largest key <= seek key if `--reverse`), output `\t`, or `END` if no match. After CURSOR_SEEK, CURSOR_NEXT advances from the seeked position and CURSOR_PREV returns the key *before* the seeked position. `CURSOR_CLOSE ` releases the cursor and its underlying snapshot. Output: `OK`. Cursors see a consistent snapshot view — expired, deleted, and range-tombstoned keys are invisible. Merged keys (from R4) return their folded value. Invalid cursor IDs return `ERROR: invalid cursor`. `STATS` adds `open_cursors`. Cursor IDs and snapshot IDs use separate ID spaces.

rounds.title: Two things this round.
rounds.md:
Two things this round.

First, rotate the WAL into segments. Instead of a single WAL file, use numbered segment files: `wal_000001.log`, `wal_000002.log`, etc. in the data directory. Start a new segment when the current one reaches or exceeds 64KB. On memtable flush, delete all WAL segments whose highest seqnum is <= the flushed seqnum — don't truncate the active segment. On startup, replay all segment files in numerical order, applying the same CRC rules per segment — a corrupted entry stops replay of that segment and all later segments are skipped entirely.

Second, there's a cursor bug. If you open a cursor with no flags, seek to a key that exists, then immediately call CURSOR_PREV, you get back the same key you just seeked to instead of the key before it. For example: PUT a, b, c, d, then open a cursor, CURSOR_SEEK to `c` returns `c	<value>`, then CURSOR_PREV should return `b	<value>` but it returns `c	<value>` again. Fix that.

rounds.title: Add online backup and restore. `BACKUP <path>` flushes the current memtable to disk, then
rounds.md:
Add online backup and restore. `BACKUP <path>` flushes the current memtable to disk, then creates a consistent point-in-time copy of the database at the given directory path. The backup contains copies of all current SSTable files plus a `backup.meta` file listing each SSTable filename, its level, and the max sequence number at backup time — one line per SSTable as `<filename> <level> <max_seqnum>`, with a header line `max_seqnum=<N>` where N is the global max seqnum. No WAL files in the backup. Output: `OK`. If the backup path already exists, overwrite it completely.

`RESTORE <path>` loads a backup: clear the entire current data directory (all SSTables, WAL segments, everything), copy the backed-up SSTables in, rebuild all in-memory state (level assignments, indexes, bloom filters) from the copied files, and set the sequence number counter to the `max_seqnum` from `backup.meta`. Output: `OK`. After RESTORE the engine continues normally — new writes get sequence numbers starting after the restored max. All open snapshots, transactions, and cursors are invalidated by RESTORE (operations on them return their respective error messages). If the backup path doesn't exist, output `ERROR: backup not found`. If the path for BACKUP already exists, overwrite it.
